title: Egyptian civilization/UEE_article 2
paragraphs: During the Byzantine period and later, the number of surviving temples was further diminished. The walls were dismantled. Limestone blocks were taken to lime kilns, while granite blocks were reused in village construction. Cities and Byzantine/Islamic-period monuments also benefited from this reuse. Mudbrick remains suffered due to the activities of the sebakhin and the deteriorating influence of the wet Delta climate. Pinedjem continued the decoration of the Khonsu temple at Karnak. During his reign, restorations were undertaken at Medinet Habu, as confirmed by the restoration texts.

The tombs were polytaphs, containing multiple burials, and after various changes served as royal caches. A temple dedicated to Khonsu may have been built north of the Amun temple at that time. Later, it was rebuilt during the 30th Dynasty, as evidenced by the discovery of statues of the god in the form of a baboon and cartouches of Psusennes I. A temple dedicated to Amun of Ipet was discovered at the end of the 20th century. It was built on the southern part of a tell, modeled after the one in Luxor.

Shoshenq I erected a temple to Amun-Ra, "Great of Fame, Lord of the Promontory," at el-Hiba/Teudjoi. They are particularly numerous during the 21st and 22nd dynasties. They have also been attested for the 26th (Saite) Dynasty, but become increasingly rare after that period. The reconstruction of the gate of Osorkon II at Bubastis is an ongoing archaeological project. The site, located in modern-day Egypt, has yielded significant findings that shed light on the life and reign of this 21st dynasty pharaoh. Osorkon II ruled from approximately 2181 to 2160 BCE and was known for his military campaigns against the Libyans and Nubians. His legacy is still visible at Bubastis, where a grand gate dedicated to him has been reconstructed. The reconstruction process involves careful excavation, documentation, and restoration of the original structure. Archaeologists have made significant progress in recent years, uncovering intricate reliefs and hieroglyphics that provide valuable insights into ancient Egyptian culture. The reconstructed gate is an impressive feat of engineering, featuring imposing stone pillars and ornate carvings. It serves as a testament to the skill and craftsmanship of the ancient Egyptians, who built this structure over 3,000 years ago. Further research and excavation are needed to fully understand the significance of Osorkon II's reign and the role of Bubastis in ancient Egyptian history. The king (or a great chief of the Meshwesh during the Libyan Period) would grant a plot of land to gate-keepers through an intermediary official.

The 25th Dynasty. The Kushite kings exercised their power primarily at Thebes, although Memphis was also the object of their attention. A copy of an ancient mythological text, known as the "Shabaqo Stone" (Junker 1940), indicates that Memphis was also a point of interest for them. In the Temple of Karnak, Taharqo built a western colonnade in the temple court west of the second pylon. Only one column remains extant today, while only the lower parts and remnants of the "screen" walls, which once connected the columns, have survived. There is no building that parallels this unique structure. We therefore have no basis for a reconstruction of the upper part.

The fundamental aspects of the theology of Amun became apparent during the Late Period and were first recorded on the walls of the Edifice of Taharqo. Montuemhat, governor of Thebes, had a restoration text inscribed in one of the smaller rooms of the Mut temple (Leclant 1961). This text describes a temple inventory conducted during the reign of Taharqo, as part of a repair program carried out at that time. The 26th Dynasty (Saite Period)

During the reign of the pharaohs from Sais, as recorded by El-Sayed in 1975, the towns of the Nile Delta underwent significant development.

Given the poor preservation of most Delta sites, we often have only the temple foundations and certain stone elements remaining. The temples of Sais also exhibit this phenomenon, although excavations have revealed very few remains in situ. In contrast, the Montu temple complex in north Karnak features several chapels erected by the same people who built their funerary chapels within the walls of Medinet Habu.

The temple courts housed an abundance of statues of priests who lived during the 26th Dynasty or slightly later, such as Neshor, Wedjahorresne, and Horkhebi (Heise 2007). Little evidence from this period has survived in the Nile Valley or the Delta. Apart from stelae found during excavations at the Suez Canal, no other significant remains have been discovered. The temple at Hibis remains the only witness to the scope and nature of building activities during the Late Period, before the start of the Ptolemaic Period.

Construction at Hibis may have begun in the 26th Dynasty, but the temple was primarily built during the succeeding 27th Dynasty and decorated mostly by Darius. The temple was completed during the rule of King Nectanebo II of the 30th Dynasty. A mudbrick temple dedicated to Osiris-iw was built in the southern part of the Kharga Oasis during the 27th Persian Dynasty.

The two kings used their royal prerogatives to establish a cult for their statues during their lifetime, sometimes under protection of the Horus falcon (De Meulenaere 1960). The art of this period is characterized as "archaizing," stemming from the search for models in the recent past, mostly those developed during the 26th Dynasty. Notably, original elements also emerge, which further develop during the subsequent period.

The processional route to Luxor, flanked by sphinxes, dates back to the Ptolemaic Period. The temple of Amun at Tanis underwent significant changes during the 30th Dynasty. A temple dedicated to Horus of Mesen was rebuilt during the Ptolemaic Period. It stood south of the temple of Amun within the enclosure wall of the 30th Dynasty, possibly linked to the nearby "East Temple". The rulers of the 30th Dynasty were active in Pi-Soped (Saft el-Henna). This is evident from the discovery of a naoi of Nectanebo I at the site. However, only a few remnants of an associated temple remain.

After the devastation that took place during the Second Persian Period, Egypt underwent a period of major development. This included the reconstruction of existing temples and the creation of new religious structures—a significant undertaking that gained momentum during the Ptolemaic Period. It should be noted that the last indigenous dynasties saw the start of the development of enormous animal necropoli, such as the Anubieion at Saqqara. This site is notable for containing tens of thousands of mummified falcons, ibises, cats, and dogs, which reflects a practice different from that of the sacred Apis cult. This practice would become especially popular in the Ptolemaic Period. We have acquired knowledge of building activities in Egypt during the first millennium BCE, thanks to excavation reports from the 19th and 20th centuries. g Badawi, 1956; Brissaud, 1987, 1998; Brissaud and Zivie-Coche, 1998, 2000; Davies, 1953; Hölscher, 1939; Naville, 1888, 1892; Parker et al., 1992; Pérez-Die and Vernus, 1992. 1979)

Arnold (1999) has compiled a synthesis that includes the Ptolemaic Period. Caution is required when using this publication, as some plans do not correspond with reality, while others are purely hypothetical.
